speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask you a few more final questions. And you may have answered it before, but I'll ask you again. Do you think of the Shoah often? Do you think of this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Holocaust--

speaker: interviewer
text: --experience? How often?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say 75% of the time probably, of my life, yeah. I-- I-- I don't have nightmares. I don't dream about it. But when I am at work sometimes, and I take a break, I think about it. Probably 50% to 75% of my life, I do think about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there something that happens during the course of the day that might trigger you to think about it more often? What-- what sort of things make you think about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I have to tell you something. Maybe it's related to what you're asking or maybe not. I'll answer your question too. You know something, through today, when I open that garage door and go in my car, I sometimes don't believe that I am free to go. I appreciate that freedom so much. When I pull out-- that happens very often. Pull out with my car from my garage, because I was confined so much to one space in the bunker there, and in the hiding places.

speaker: interviewee
text: Today I look out and I think-- I breathe, ah, the fresh air. It's wonderful. My God, am I allowed to get out of here? And here I say, I am so lucky I can get out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then you know, life has a different meaning to me than to most people, I think. On days like this-- that doesn't happen every day, of course. But just the other day, it happened. I went out. My God, I am free. I can go. That's a wonderful thing. I appreciate it so much. And-- and-- and I had to tell you that, because that's what I felt.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's very important.

speaker: interviewee
text: You asked me a question. I'm sorry about that. What did you ask me about?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I wanted to know--

speaker: interviewee
text: That one thing triggers more than another--

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how-- yeah, what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Doesn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: How often do you think about it? What kinds of things might trigger thoughts about what happened in the past?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There's no single thing that I can answer that does that to me. It just comes on when it comes on. You know, how your thoughts are.

speaker: interviewer
text: You think about maybe, California when you-- when you're here sometimes. It just comes on automatically. Thoughts go far away. You go to the old country, go to-- you can do a lot with thoughts, you know? And it just-- just happens. When you wake up in the morning--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --do you always know where you're at?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. That doesn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Not like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: --affect me that way. No, I don't get-- like I said, no real nightmares or anything like that like. I am lucky in that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you used to have nightmares?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but it was-- the feelings were a lot stronger right after the war than they are now. Time has-- time to heal a little bit. I don't live with it all my life, but still the thoughts are there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why-- why did you want to tell your Story

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the main reason is because I wanted-- you said that this is going in the archives. And people want to know what was happening. And I'm the witness to what was there. I mean, witness, me and my family. And I wanted to make-- if-- when I die, I mean, there's nobody firsthand that can tell the story anymore. That's number one. Number two, I was thinking about maybe I believe in God. There is something superior being. There is God. Maybe God wanted me to be alive so I can tell some story about it. Maybe that's the reason for me being alive. And then there is a guilt feeling.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is true with many others. I talk to others. Why am I alive? Why are they dead? Am I-- am I better than them? I am not better than my cousins were. And they're all dead. And why am I alive? Maybe they wanted me to be alive to tell that story about them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe that's what I'm-- a spokesman for my-- [SOBBING] this is family. Maybe that's what I'm here for. Maybe that's what God intended. If there is God, in heaven, maybe that's what he wanted.

speaker: interviewee
text: So those are several reasons why I am here today. Even though it's emotional. But I think I had to get it off and-- and tell you the story. How-- how do you feel about the way that you've lived your life since the war? How do you-- how do--

speaker: interviewee
text: Since after the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, how do you feel about how you lived your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't think it will ever be normal, what you call, quotation marks, 'normal.' We're always going to have that injustice in our mind. We, the survivors, I'm talking about. We cannot possibly lead a completely normal life. When you look at people with criticism like that, which when I was a kid, everybody was a friend. Everybody was nice. It was Utopia, almost. The Polacks were nice to us. Everybody was nice, which wouldn't be now, if I would go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know? And it's-- you look with different eyes at things after you go through a thing like that. So that's one mark that-- but that's a small price to pay, I guess. Do you feel your experience changed the way that you raised your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I-- you're right about-- I was thinking about where was I, when they grew up and they were like-- when I was their age, how lucky we are that they are free. You cannot imagine. I appreciate the freedom more-- probably more than any average person, or average American, or anybody that knows they have freedom. And I probably appreciate it 10 times more. I have to say that. Because that's what I feel. When I get out of the garage and feel freedom, that means a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not everybody feels that way about it, do they? That's-- it's granted. it comes-- you-- you go out on the street, and you-- you got freedom. It's nothing. It's just-- it's come-- coming to you, isn't it?

speaker: interviewee
text: But it isn't. It isn't coming to you. We saw that at the time. That's why my life is different, probably than-- a little bit different than most average persons. You know what I mean. Maybe I didn't express it too well.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know--

speaker: interviewee
text: But you know what I'm talking about.

speaker: interviewer
text: --I think I do. Have there been any decisions that you've made or-- or things that you have done in your life that were influenced by your Shoah experience? Did you do things differently?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a very difficult question. Because I don't know how I would have behaved if the Shoah was not there. We behaved differently towards people or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Towards people--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I told you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Towards your own family.

speaker: interviewee
text: My own family. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't think I would have done things-- It's hard to guess what-- or speculate, huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess-- I-- I am a compassionate person. I love my family dearly. And they just-- I don't-- I appreciate it a lot. Maybe I appreciate them a little more than anybody else. Maybe we are closer. I don't-- I don't know. I can't attribute it to the Holocaust, or the-- the Shoah, whatever. I don't know why. I-- just, we are a very close-knit family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think for the Jews now, in the world, do you think what happened 50 years ago could happen again?

speaker: interviewee
text: That entered my mind, that this thing can happen again. What-- look what happened in Bosnia. You know, I felt for those people, that they were exterminated like that because they were a certain race. That is wrong. That is completely wrong, what they did there. Those people that-- they wanted to live too. You know, that affected me, when I heard that. Something should-- we-- I don't think we, as Americans, did enough. I don't think we did enough. I don't think an average American appreciate what was happening. And I can-- I can see, well, the people were in misery, when they were shot in ditches there because of their religion, because of their belief. That's the only reason why. So in that respect, my life, maybe, and the attitude towards people change a little bit. I believe that the Americans-- that the Americans could have down a little more to prevent some of the disasters. But it's difficult to explain to somebody that didn't go through it. I can understand that too. I can appreciate it. And if I was born here and all the time I was raised here, and as an American, maybe I would be the same way. I don't know if I would be different. You know, you can't blame American people for that. Everybody wants peace. Most people do want peace.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever talk to other groups about your experience and what happened? And tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. And the Glen Oak High School, they invited me to talk about it. I talked about it there for-- about an hour.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were different questions. And what I resented so much when they said, how do you know this was all-- how do we know that this was all true, what you're telling us? I said, I cannot tell you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You just have to believe me, what I tell you. I came from there and I'm a survivor. And I'm telling you my experience. I have no reason to make up stories like that. That's all I told them. But there was a couple guys, and that hurt me a lot too. And then I talked in the shul there. I talked to the kids there in the-- the Hebrew school. I talked, made a couple talks. Otherwise, that's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I said, before in the beginning, we are still considered fortunate in this misfortune of ours, that I didn't have to be in the concentration camp. So I'm still fortunate in that respect. From what I hear what was happening there, it's almost unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your reaction to

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- --very well done. It was highlights. Of course they couldn't show more. It was just scraping the surface. What you saw there was not near what was happening. It was just barely-- they just scraped the surface there. But that's all you could do in a movie.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't know during-- while-- I mean, while that was going on and you were in hiding, those kinds of things--

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not know the details.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't know--

speaker: interviewee
text: How?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: No newspaper, no news. Nobody knows anything about it. They kept it a secret between themselves. The Germans kept it a secret. They tried to keep it a secret and especially inside there-- you-- you-- we didn't know anything about concentration camps or anything like that, till after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How are you feeling now? You told your story here. How--

speaker: interviewee
text: How do I feel? After I told it, I feel very heavy, like I-- I'll get over it. It's important what-- I feel like it's a little load off my mind, that I could tell you the story. And with you here, that people would know about it. And the best of my recollection, things happened exactly the way I told you. Maybe some year or month or to date wasn't exactly right. But that was almost 50-some years ago. But otherwise, all the details are facts. That happened to me. And more, that you can't really tell all the details, what was happening. What you lived through the-- the-- that you-- that you're scared all the time. For example, in the woods we were there. People were go out hunt-- hunting. And they hunt. We didn't know it was Germans or was hunters. We didn't know who it was, why they were shooting the gun off. We were trembling. We were scared.

speaker: interviewer
text: What could you do? That-- to live like that, you get conditioned to it after a while, though. You're scared all the time. Sounds like you truly appreciate freedom now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Freedom is a wonderful thing. And I don't take it for granted. It's the greatest thing in the world. I do appreciate it. I think about it a lot. I think about it a lot, really. It's a wonderful thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you call yourself a patriotic American?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I love America. It's a beautiful country, one of the best that I know. And they try-- there is someone unfairness in this country, like everywhere else. But at least the government is trying to get everybody equal and fair. I appreciate that a lot, more than most people. That-- that's how lucky-- I-- I-- I love America. I love her a lot-- America a lot. And they help us out too. And I just-- wonderful country, believe me, we live in a wonderful country.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I am-- as far as that goes, I am patriotic about it. I am. And I'm probably nationalistic about Israel too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Both countries, they're just great. It's just some people that make it bad. It's not the regime and not the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll go take a look at the pictures here in a moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, you're on camera. Both of you now. Camera's rolling. Ed, can you tell us who the lovely lady is on your right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's my lifelong girlfriend. That's my wife, Rita. And we've been married now since 1958. So that makes it about 38 years. RITA (WIFE): Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rita, can you tell us, when where were you married to Ed? RITA (WIFE): Exactly June, 29th, 1958, here in Canton, Ohio, at the Garden Center, in the park. OK. RITA (WIFE): And it's hard to believe that that much time has passed. Can you tell us about some of your reactions to this whole process, and what you hope to accomplish by doing this? or-- RITA (WIFE): Well, I think it's a good idea that they are promoting this and it'll be in the archives for people to see for many years to come. Of course, I don't have-- I can't naturally have the same feelings that somebody who's gone through this has. But I think that, you know, it should never be forgotten. OK. I think that's-- that's enough of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling? OK, can you tell us, Ed, who is this picture here, where it was taken and who it is?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken around our DP camp in Furth, by Nuremberg. And the lady sitting there, her name is Ann Goldman. She was a girlfriend of the fellow on my left, on our left. She was going with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am on the right side, extreme right. That's me. And in the center is a fellow-- was one of the wonderful friends of mine, Abrashka Rifkind. He is a survivor too. And he was one of my best friends. He is a jeweler. He makes-- he makes watches. He buys watches and stuff like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the guy on the left side is called Michael Selzer. He lives now in Buffalo, New York. And I visited him a couple times. Really good friends, those are my best friends that I had from Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK, who is this?

speaker: interviewer
text: That's another one.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is myself, Edward Applebaum. And I don't remember exactly when the picture was taken. But I must be about 20, 21 when I came here. I think is it. We don't have any pictures from before the war. They were all destroyed. So all the pictures were-- it was after '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Ed, tell us who is in this picture and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- in the front, the little kid is my sister, baby sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Annette. ?] And she was born after the war in 1945. And that's her mom sitting on-- to her right-- or left. That's Sally. And to my right is my father, Samuel. And then in the background, their heads are cut off some. But that's-- on the left is my brother, Bernard. And on the right, that's me. Half of my face is off. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, tell us who is in this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, on our left is me. It's Edward. And my father is on the right, Samuel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think this was-- must have been taken in Canton, Ohio, in 1949 or '50.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, of course, this picture is my baby sister. There is 18 years difference between us. Her name is [? Annette. ?] And that's me next to her, Edward. Must have been done 19-- around 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, can you tell us, who is this, Ed?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my late mother, Sally. And then with her back to us, that's my baby sister again, about 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK. Can you tell me who's in this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father when I think he was working for the Bachsbaum factory here in Canton. And they had a little picnic with his core employees. I don't know who those people are. But he is in the forefront, my dad, Samuel. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in the background?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Some of his friends that worked with him. I don't know who they are.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --so they were Jewish, I'm sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Rita, tell us who's in this picture, from left to right.

speaker: interviewee
text: RITA (WIFE): From left to right is my husband, Edward, our younger son, Gordon, myself, our older son, Dennis, my sister-in-law, Ed's sister, [? Annette, ?] and her husband, Richard. They live in St. Louis. This was a special occasion of our younger son's-- Gordon's-- graduation from medical school. And we just took a little family picture here. We had about 200 people there for a brunch at the Park Hotel. That was in 1989.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you very--
